medications.drug_name: oral rehydration solution (ORS) — small frequent sips
medications.indication_rationale: The mainstay — replaces fluid/electrolyte losses; give small amounts frequently to avoid triggering vomiting. Source advised 5–10 mL every 5 minutes by spoon.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5–10 mL every ~5 minutes, increasing as tolerated (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A single antiemetic can break the vomiting cycle so oral rehydration succeeds; source gave ondansetron 4mg SL stat.

medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally every 6 hours (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Routine antidiarrhoeals can worsen invasive infection and antibiotics are not indicated for viral gastroenteritis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rehydrate with ORS by small frequent sips; rest; reintroduce bland food as tolerated; strict hand hygiene and don't prepare food for others while symptomatic.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintains hydration without triggering vomiting and prevents onward spread.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use ORS rather than plain water alone to replace salts.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Plain water alone does not replace electrolyte losses.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral tummy bug (gastroenteritis). The vomiting usually settles within a day and the diarrhoea over a few days. The key is to keep fluids in — take small sips of rehydration solution every few minutes (the anti-nausea wafer will help). Antibiotics and anti-diarrhoea tablets aren't needed.
Use rehydration salts (not just plain water), rest, and wash your hands well; don't prepare food for others while you're unwell.

Call 000 / go to ED if you get severe constant or one-sided tummy pain or a rigid tender abdomen, blood in the stool or vomit, you stop passing urine or become very lightheaded/faint, a high fever develops, or you still can't keep any fluids down.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Hydration, ongoing diarrhoea; stool tests if bloody/high fever/travel/prolonged.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Surgical-abdomen features / failed rehydration / bloody stool — ED.

must_include_items: Screen surgical abdomen (appendicitis/obstruction) and invasive-diarrhoea features
Oral rehydration (small frequent sips of ORS) ± antiemetic
acceptable_alternatives: Ondansetron/metoclopramide antiemetic; ORS; paracetamol for fever
Telehealth/GP follow-up; stool tests if indicated
errors_of_commission: Advising plain-water-only rehydration as the key measure
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a surgical/invasive screen, oral-rehydration-led management (avoiding antidiarrhoeals/antibiotics), and dehydration + surgical safety-nets.
